notes: Integrated from the real Grade 2 Quarter 2, Book 8 source file.
Strong Reader card: longer text and more inferential fable work.

book.title: The Crow and the Pitcher
book.description: The Crow and the Pitcher includes a six-chapter reading sequence, vocabulary, comprehension questions, journal/writing prompts, and assessment checks.

choiceGuidance.reason: longer text and more inferential fable work

weeks.title: The Crow and the Pitcher Book Card
vocabulary: 1. mercilessly - without pity or compassion; harshly
2. quench - to satisfy thirst by drinking
1. immovable - impossible to move or change
2. exploit - to make use of a situation or weakness
1. tantalizingly - in a way that is tempting but out of reach
2. debris - scattered pieces of waste or remains
1. persistence - continuing to try despite difficulties
2. skepticism - doubt about whether something will work
1. accumulated - collected or gathered over time
1. boastful - showing off or bragging about achievements
2. obstacles - things that block progress or make something difficult
journal: Write directions for how the crow solves the problem.
Explain how patience and thinking help the crow.
writing: Write directions for how the crow solves the problem.
Explain how patience and thinking help the crow.
questions: 1. What was the crow searching for on the hot summer day?
2. What problem did the crow discover with the pitcher?
1. What did the crow try to do to reach the water?
2. What was the crow known for throughout the forest?
1. What did the crow notice around the base of the oak tree?
2. What was the crow's brilliant idea?
1. What happened each time the crow dropped a stone in the pitcher?
2. How did the crow respond to the other animals' doubts?
1. What happened as more stones accumulated in the pitcher?
2. Why did the water taste so delicious to the crow?
1. What did the crow teach the other animals about problem-solving?
2. What lesson did the crow's story teach throughout the forest?
assessment: Use chapter comprehension questions for daily checks.
Use journal/writing prompt as written-response evidence.
